title: Implement registry storage and register/list commands
description: Build versioned fleet registry YAML I/O, filtering, duplicate/path validation, health checks for listed projects, and the `register`, `unregister`, and `list` CLI commands.
acceptance_criteria: Registry load/write behaviour matches the versioning and empty-registry rules in the spec.
Project registration canonicalises absolute paths, rejects duplicates, and supports optional names and tags.
`list` supports tag filtering, plain output, and computes `missing`, `no-takt`, `takt-error`, and `ok` health values in the specified order.
Registry writes are atomic and confined to fleet-owned config paths.

handoff_summary.completed: Implemented registry.py with load_registry/save_registry (atomic write via temp+rename), filter_projects (AND-tag, OR-name), compute_health (missing→no-takt→takt-error→ok), and RegistryError. Implemented formatters.py with format_table and format_project_list. Implemented cli/commands/register.py with command_register (canonicalise path, reject duplicates, warn on no-.takt/), command_unregister (match by name or path), command_list (tag filtering, health computation, plain/formatted output). Created tests/fleet/test_registry.py (20 tests covering load, save, filter, health) and tests/fleet/test_cli_register.py (16 tests covering all three commands).
handoff_summary.remaining: Downstream beads must implement the other command modules: dispatch.py, run.py, summary.py, watch.py, runs.py. The boundary test file (test_boundaries.py) referenced in the spec's acceptance criteria is also pending.
handoff_summary.risks: The `test_list_tag_filter` test uses a manual print-capture approach (patching builtins.print) rather than capsys because the registry_path patch and command_list share the same mock scope — a minor brittleness, but functionally correct. Health check runs a real subprocess in production; tests mock subprocess.run throughout.
handoff_summary.next_action: Tester agent should run the full test suite (pytest tests/) and verify: load/save/filter/health unit tests, CLI command tests, and that the existing agent_takt tests are not regressed.
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: formatters.py is a new file; no other in-progress bead claims it. registry.py is also new. No overlap with existing agent_takt files.
handoff_summary.design_decisions: 1. Atomic write via tempfile+os.replace: guarantees readers always see a complete YAML document — matches the spec's explicit requirement and mirrors safe_dump-to-temp pattern common in config writers. Alternative (write directly) risks a partial file on crash. 2. compute_health runs subprocess inline (blocking): the spec says health is computed fresh on each list invocation; no caching added since the spec does not ask for it and the command has no parallelism requirement. 3. filter_projects is AND-semantics for tags, OR-semantics for names: matches the spec's filter contract exactly. 4. format_project_list accepts a pre-computed health_map dict rather than calling compute_health internally: keeps formatters.py a pure presentation layer with no subprocess dependency — easier to test and consistent with the spec's boundary model (writes are confined; formatters just render). 5. Plain output uses tabs (not spaces): makes awk/cut parsing unambiguous regardless of cell width.
handoff_summary.test_coverage_notes: test_registry.py: covers load for missing file, valid file, empty projects, missing version, higher version, non-integer version, absent tags; save roundtrip, version field presence, parent dir creation; filter by single tag, multi-tag AND, name OR, combined, no-match; health for missing path, no-.takt/, .takt/ without config.yaml, non-zero exit, timeout, and ok. test_cli_register.py: covers register success, custom name, tags, nonexistent path, duplicate rejection, warning on no-.takt/; unregister by name, by path, not-found, removes-only-target; list empty, shows projects, plain output, tag filtering, health column. Tester should also verify that takt-fleet list --plain emits tab-separated rows without a separator line.
handoff_summary.known_limitations: The test_list_tag_filter test uses a manual builtins.print patch to capture output rather than pytest capsys — this is because the registry_path patch is already active in that scope and capsys works at the file-descriptor level. This is functional but somewhat brittle; the tester agent may want to restructure this test using a simpler approach (e.g. calling command_list with a StringIO-redirected stdout). No other known gaps.

conflict_risks: formatters.py is a new file; no other in-progress bead claims it. registry.py is also new. No overlap with existing agent_takt files.

execution_history.summary: Implemented fleet registry storage (registry.py), table/list formatters (formatters.py), and the register/unregister/list CLI commands (cli/commands/register.py), plus full test coverage in tests/fleet/test_registry.py and tests/fleet/test_cli_register.py. All files pass syntax check and imports resolve correctly.

guardrails.template_text:
# Developer Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Implement only the assigned bead inside the existing architecture and file scope.

Allowed actions:
- Modify code, configuration, or local docs required to complete the assigned bead.
- Create sub-beads for discovered follow-up work that should be handled separately.
- Verify your `{{LANGUAGE}}` changes compile and import correctly with a quick syntax check: `{{BUILD_CHECK_COMMAND}}`.

Disallowed actions:
- Run any test suite — not even a targeted `{{TEST_COMMAND}}` run or any subset. Test execution is exclusively the tester agent's responsibility. Running tests wastes the agent budget and delays the pipeline.
- Redesign unrelated architecture or broaden scope beyond the assigned bead.
- Perform final review signoff in place of a review agent.
- Silently absorb unrelated test, documentation, or planning work that should be handed off.

## Memory

**Read memory at bead start.** Before touching any code, run three searches using `$TAKT_CMD` (injected by the orchestrator):

```bash
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace global
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace feature:<feature_root_id>
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace specs
```

Treat results as ambient context — apply relevant entries; skip entries that don't apply.

**Write to memory when you discover reusable project knowledge** — something that would have changed your approach if you had known it upfront, and is not already in CLAUDE.md or the guardrails.

```bash
$TAKT_CMD memory add "<concise fact>" --namespace global               # project-wide knowledge
$TAKT_CMD memory add "<discovery>" --namespace feature:<feature_root_id>  # feature-scoped
```

Required Handoff Fields:

Every developer bead **must** populate the following three fields in its output JSON. Reviewers and tester agents rely on these fields to scope their work without additional back-and-forth turns. Use `"N/A"` only when a field is genuinely inapplicable (e.g., a trivial rename with no design trade-offs); do not leave fields blank.

- **`design_decisions`** — Document non-obvious architectural or implementation choices made during this bead. Include alternatives considered and why the chosen approach was preferred. Reviewers use this to evaluate correctness without re-deriving intent. Set to `"N/A"` only if the change was purely mechanical with no meaningful trade-offs.

- **`test_coverage_notes`** — Describe what the tester agent should verify: new code paths, edge cases introduced, regression risks, and any known untested scenarios left to the tester. Set to `"N/A"` only if the bead touched no executable code paths (e.g., documentation-only changes).

- **`known_limitations`** — Call out constraints, deferred work, or known gaps in the implementation. This includes out-of-scope items deliberately left for follow-up beads and any areas where the implementation is intentionally incomplete. Set to `"N/A"` if the implementation is complete as specified with no known gaps.

Expected outputs:
- Completed or blocked JSON with concise implementation summary.
- Accurate touched files, changed files, risks, and follow-up handoff fields.
- Populated `design_decisions`, `test_coverage_notes`, and `known_limitations` fields in every response.
